join_order.description: Subquery decorrelation: scan all lineitem, accumulate sum(l_quantity) per l_orderkey into hash map, filter to orderkeys where sum > 300 (scaled: > 30000). Result: compact hash set of ~57 qualifying orderkeys.
join_order.build: lineitem(full scan, group by l_orderkey, HAVING SUM(l_quantity*100) > 30000)

join_order.description: Scan orders (15M rows), semi-join probe against qualifying_orderkeys set (~57 entries). Collect ~57 matching order rows with o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice.
For each of the ~57 qualifying orders, lookup customer name via prebuilt idx_customer_custkey hash index (o_custkey -> c_name). No scan needed — point lookups on ~57 keys.
For each of the ~57 qualifying orderkeys, use prebuilt idx_lineitem_orderkey (hash multi-value) to fetch all lineitem rows for those orderkeys. Sum l_quantity per orderkey. No full lineitem re-scan.

subquery_detail.implementation: Single parallel scan of lineitem (60M rows). Thread-local hash maps accumulate l_orderkey -> sum(l_quantity). Merge thread-local maps. Filter entries where sum > 30000 (300 * scale_factor=100). Result inserted into compact_hash_set<int32_t> of qualifying orderkeys.
subquery_detail.scale_factor_note: l_quantity stored as int64_t with scale_factor=100. Threshold 300 must be compared as 30000 against raw stored values.

aggregation_detail.strategy: flat_array_or_tiny_hash_map — with only ~57 groups, a simple vector of structs is optimal. Accumulate sum_qty while fetching lineitem rows via index in step 4.

filter_pushdown.note: ~57 qualifying orderkeys out of ~8M distinct orderkeys in lineitem

late_materialization.detail: c_name (STRING) loaded only for the ~57 qualifying orders via idx_customer_custkey point lookup. Avoids scanning 1.5M customer name strings.

data_structures.subquery_lineitem_agg: thread_local compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t>(pre_sized, ~8M capacity) per thread, merge after parallel scan. Then filter to qualifying set.
data_structures.qualifying_orderkeys: compact_hash_set<int32_t>(capacity=128) — ~57 entries, fits in a single cache line region. O(1) probe during orders scan.
data_structures.orders_semijoin: no hash table built — probe qualifying_orderkeys set during orders scan, collect matching rows into result_vector<OrderRow>(~57 entries)
data_structures.customer_lookup: prebuilt_index_load(idx_customer_custkey) — mmap hash index, O(1) point lookup per qualifying order
data_structures.lineitem_index_lookup: prebuilt_index_load(idx_lineitem_orderkey) — mmap hash multi-value index, fetch all line rows for each of ~57 orderkeys
data_structures.aggregation: vector<ResultRow> of ~57 entries — accumulate sum_qty directly during lineitem index fetch; no hash table needed at this cardinality

phases.note: 64 threads scan 60M lineitem rows in morsels of 64K. Each thread maintains local compact_hash_map<int32_t,int64_t> for orderkey->sum_qty. After parallel scan, sequentially merge 64 local maps (merge is fast: ~8M entries total). Filter qualifying entries (sum > 30000) into compact_hash_set.
64 threads scan 15M orders rows. Probe qualifying_orderkeys set (read-only, shared, fits in L1 cache). Matching rows (~57) written to thread-local vectors; merge after parallel phase.
Only ~57 qualifying orders. Point lookups into prebuilt customer and lineitem indexes. Sequential is sufficient — negligible time.
~57 result rows. std::partial_sort for top 100 (trivially a full sort at this scale).

parallelism.synchronization: thread_local_merge — each thread owns its local aggregation buffer; no contention during scan phases. Merge is sequential post-scan.

index_usage.purpose: Point lookup: o_custkey -> c_name for ~57 qualifying orders. Load via mmap. O(1) per lookup.
Multi-value fetch: l_orderkey -> all lineitem rows for the ~57 qualifying orderkeys. Avoids second full 60M-row lineitem scan. O(result_size) per qualifying orderkey.

scan_detail.pass_1: Full parallel scan of lineitem (60M rows) — columns: l_orderkey, l_quantity only. Build subquery hash map and qualifying set.
scan_detail.pass_2: Full parallel scan of orders (15M rows) — columns: o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice. Semi-join probe against qualifying_orderkeys.
scan_detail.no_scan: customer and lineitem (second access) — served entirely via prebuilt index point lookups, no column file scan needed.

memory_detail.lineitem_scan: Sequential streaming read of l_orderkey.bin and l_quantity.bin (2 columns × 60M rows × ~8 bytes = ~960MB). HDD prefetch benefits from sequential access.
memory_detail.orders_scan: Sequential streaming read of o_orderkey.bin, o_custkey.bin, o_orderdate.bin, o_totalprice.bin (4 columns × 15M rows = ~240MB).
memory_detail.index_lookups: Random access via mmap into idx_customer_custkey and idx_lineitem_orderkey. Only ~57 lookups — negligible I/O.

estimated_cost.target_comparison: DuckDB=175ms, Umbra=180ms — target sub-150ms via parallel lineitem scan + prebuilt index elimination of second lineitem full scan
